title: Near-Optimal Primal-Dual Algorithm for Learning Linear Mixture CMDPs with Adversarial Rewards
abstract: We study safe reinforcement learning in finite-horizon linear mixture constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs) with adversarial rewards under full-information feedback and an unknown transition kernel. We propose a primal-dual policy optimization algorithm that achieves regret and constraint violation bounds of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2 H^3 K})$ under mild conditions, where $d$ is the feature dimension, $H$ is the horizon, and $K$ is the number of episodes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first provably efficient algorithm for linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards. In particular, our regret bound is near-optimal, matching the known minimax lower bound up to logarithmic factors. The key idea is to introduce a regularized dual update that enables a drift-based analysis. This step is essential, as strong duality-based analysis cannot be directly applied when reward functions change across episodes. In addition, we extend weighted ridge regression-based parameter estimation to the constrained setting, allowing us to construct tighter confidence intervals that are crucial for deriving the near-optimal regret bound.

sections.heading: IIntroduction
sections.text:
In this paper, we study online linear mixture constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs) with adversarial rewards. A CMDP can be described by the following standard formulation: 1 1 1 We note that ( 1 ) represents the standard CMDP formulation; the formal definition of linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards will be introduced in Section II . max π ⁡ V 1 r , π ​ ( s 1 ) subject to V 1 g , π ​ ( s 1 ) ≥ b , \max_{\pi}\ V_{1}^{r,\pi}(s_{1})\quad\textnormal{subject to}\quad V_{1}^{g,\pi}(s_{1})\geq b, (1) where π \pi denotes a policy, V 1 r , π ​ ( s 1 ) = 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ h = 1 H r h ​ ( s h , a h ) ∣ s 1 ] V_{1}^{r,\pi}(s_{1})=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_{h=1}^{H}r_{h}(s_{h},a_{h})\mid s_{1}] , V 1 g , π ​ ( s 1 ) = 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ h = 1 H g h ​ ( s h , a h ) ∣ s 1 ] V_{1}^{g,\pi}(s_{1})=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\sum_{h=1}^{H}g_{h}(s_{h},a_{h})\mid s_{1}] , and b b denotes the constraint threshold.

Recently, a large number of algorithms for learning CMDPs have been proposed [ 9 ] . However, many existing approaches are limited to settings with tabular state spaces [ 20 ] , and their theoretical guarantees do not readily extend to large state spaces. To address scalability, one common direction in RL is to consider linear function approximation, where transition kernels admit a linear representation. In the CMDP literature, [ 6 , 10 , 12 , 18 ] proposed algorithms in this setting; however, these works considered fixed rewards and constraints and thus could not capture non-stationary environments. To address this limitation, more recently, [ 8 , 37 ] proposed algorithms for non-stationary environments; however, [ 8 ] required the variation budget—a quantity that characterizes the change in a CMDP—to be known and bounded, and [ 37 ] only attained suboptimal regret and violation bounds. In summary, the existing algorithms for safe RL with linear function approximation either suffer from suboptimal regret guarantees or rely on restrictive assumptions, such as stationarity or a bounded variation budget.

Motivated by these limitations, we design an algorithm for linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards that achieves near-optimal regret bounds. In this setting, the linear mixture structure enables function approximation, while adversarial rewards capture non-stationary environments.

This goal is particularly challenging. Specifically, when rewards change arbitrarily, strong duality-based analysis—a key tool in previous works [ 6 , 10 ] —cannot be directly applied. Moreover, achieving the optimal sample complexity requires constructing tighter confidence intervals. Despite these challenges, our main contributions are as follows. • We propose a provably efficient primal-dual policy optimization algorithm for finite-horizon linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial reward functions under full-information feedback, a fixed constraint, and an unknown transition kernel. • Our algorithm achieves regret and constraint violation bounds of O ~ ​ ( d 2 ​ H 3 ​ K ) \widetilde{{O}}(\sqrt{d^{2}H^{3}K}) . Here, d d is the feature dimension, H H is the horizon, and K K is the number of episodes. We emphasize that our regret bound is near-optimal in the sense that it matches the known minimax regret lower bound for unconstrained linear mixture MDPs up to logarithmic factors [ 14 ] . • The key idea of our algorithm is to combine primal-dual policy optimization with (i) a regularized dual update that enables a drift-based analysis, which does not rely on strong duality, and (ii) weighted ridge regression–based parameter estimation to obtain tighter confidence intervals, which are crucial for attaining near-optimal regret bounds.

In Table I , we compare algorithms for safe RL with linear function approximation. [ 11 , 21 , 35 ] consider infinite-horizon linear CMDPs, and [ 31 ] studies q π q^{\pi} -realizable CMDPs. Although these works fall under safe RL with linear function approximation, their settings differ significantly from ours.

sections.heading: I-AAdditional Related Works
sections.text: We provide additional prior works on learning CMDPs. [ 32 , 4 , 38 , 22 , 40 , 28 , 19 ] propose online algorithms for tabular CMDPs. [ 23 , 29 , 27 , 30 ] are particularly relevant to our work as they consider adversarial rewards; however, their results are restricted to the tabular setting. Beyond the tabular case, several works study CMDPs with function approximation. In particular, [ 2 , 26 , 33 , 24 ] focus on instantaneous constraints, where the constraint must be satisfied at each step rather than in expectation over the entire trajectory.

sections.heading: IIProblem Formulation
sections.text:
Notation. For a positive integer n n , let [ n ] = { 1 , … , n } [n]=\{1,\ldots,n\} . For x ∈ ℝ d x\in\mathbb{R}^{d} , let ‖ x ‖ A = x ⊤ ​ A ​ x \|x\|_{A}=\sqrt{x^{\top}Ax} for some positive definite matrix A ∈ ℝ d × d A\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d} . For a , b ∈ ℝ a,b\in\mathbb{R} such that a ≤ b a\leq b , let [ a , b ] = { x ∈ ℝ : a ≤ x ≤ b } [a,b]=\{x\in\mathbb{R}:a\leq x\leq b\} , let [ ⋅ ] + = max ⁡ { ⋅ , 0 } [\cdot]_{+}=\max\{\cdot,0\} , and let [ ⋅ ] [ a , b ] = max ⁡ { min ⁡ { ⋅ , b } , a } [\cdot]_{[a,b]}=\max\{\min\{\cdot,b\},a\} . Let I ∈ ℝ d × d I\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d} denote the identity matrix, let 𝟎 ∈ ℝ d \bm{0}\in\mathbb{R}^{d} denote the all-0 vector, and let ⟨ ⋅ , ⋅ ⟩ \langle\cdot,\cdot\rangle denote the inner product. Let Δ ​ ( A ) \Delta(A) denote the probability simplex over set A A .

Finite-Horizon CMDP. We consider a finite-horizon CMDP with adversarial rewards and a fixed constraint function. A finite-horizon CMDP is defined by ℳ = ( S , A , H , P , r , g , b , s 1 ) {\mathcal{M}}=(S,A,H,P,r,g,b,s_{1}) , where S S is the finite state space, A A is the finite action space, and H H is the horizon. The collection of unknown transition kernels is denoted by P = { P h } h ∈ [ H ] P=\{P_{h}\}_{h\in[H]} , where P h ​ ( s ′ | s , a ) P_{h}(s^{\prime}|s,a) is the probability of transitioning from state s s to state s ′ s^{\prime} by taking action a a at step h h . The collections of reward and constraint functions are denoted by r = { r h k } h ∈ [ H ] , k ∈ [ K ] r=\{r_{h}^{k}\}_{h\in[H],k\in[K]} and g = { g h } h ∈ [ H ] g=\{g_{h}\}_{h\in[H]} , where r h k , g h : S × A → [ 0 , 1 ] r_{h}^{k},g_{h}:S\times A\to[0,1] for each h , k h,k . The constraint threshold is denoted by b ∈ [ 0 , H ] b\in[0,H] , and s 1 s_{1} is the fixed initial state.

We assume adversarial deterministic rewards with full-information feedback, while the deterministic constraint function is fixed and known. 2 2 2 As in [ 6 , 10 ] , we assume the constraint function is fixed and deterministic for simplicity. Our results can be extended to an unknown stochastic constraint function with linear structure under bandit feedback. . In particular, at the beginning of each episode k k , an adversary chooses an arbitrary reward function r k = { r h k } h ∈ [ H ] r^{k}=\{r_{h}^{k}\}_{h\in[H]} . The full information of r k r^{k} is revealed at the end of episode k k . On the other hand, the constraint function g = { g h } h ∈ [ H ] g=\{g_{h}\}_{h\in[H]} is assumed to be fixed across all k ∈ [ K ] k\in[K] and known.

The agent interacts with the environment as follows. At the beginning of episode k k , the agent selects a policy { π h k } h ∈ [ H ] \{\pi_{h}^{k}\}_{h\in[H]} , where π h k ​ ( a | s ) \pi_{h}^{k}(a|s) denotes the probability of taking action a a at state s s and step h h . During episode k k , for each step h h , the agent observes the current state s h k s_{h}^{k} and selects an action a h k ∼ π h k ( ⋅ | s h k ) a_{h}^{k}\sim\pi_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s_{h}^{k}) . The next state is then sampled as s h + 1 k ∼ P h ( ⋅ | s h k , a h k ) s_{h+1}^{k}\sim P_{h}(\cdot|s_{h}^{k},a_{h}^{k}) . At the end of the episode, r k r^{k} is revealed to the agent.

Given P P and π \pi , we define the value function V h ℓ , π ​ ( s ) = 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ j = h H ℓ j ​ ( s j , a j ) | s h = s ] , V_{h}^{\ell,\pi}(s)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\![\sum_{j=h}^{H}\ell_{j}(s_{j},a_{j})|s_{h}=s], where ℓ = { ℓ h } h ∈ [ H ] \ell=\{\ell_{h}\}_{h\in[H]} is any function such that ℓ h : S × A → [ 0 , 1 ] \ell_{h}:S\times A\to[0,1] , and 𝔼 π ​ [ ⋅ ] \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[\cdot] denotes the expectation over the trajectory ( s h , a h , … , s H , a H ) (s_{h},a_{h},\ldots,s_{H},a_{H}) induced by P P and π \pi . Likewise, we define the Q Q -function as Q h ℓ , π ​ ( s , a ) = 𝔼 π ​ [ ∑ j = h H ℓ j ​ ( s j , a j ) | s h = s , a h = a ] . Q_{h}^{\ell,\pi}(s,a)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\![\sum_{j=h}^{H}\ell_{j}(s_{j},a_{j})|s_{h}=s,a_{h}=a].

The goal of the agent is to learn an optimal policy π ∗ ∈ Π \pi^{*}\in\Pi for the following optimization problem, where for the set of policies Π = { { π h } h ∈ [ H ] : π h : S → Δ ​ ( A ) } \Pi=\{\{\pi_{h}\}_{h\in[H]}:\pi_{h}:S\to\Delta(A)\} : max π ∈ Π ​ ∑ k = 1 K V 1 r k , π ​ ( s 1 ) subject to V 1 g , π ​ ( s 1 ) ≥ b . \max_{\pi\in\Pi}\ \sum_{k=1}^{K}V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi}(s_{1})\quad\textnormal{subject to}\quad V_{1}^{g,\pi}(s_{1})\geq b. (2)

The performance metrics are defined as follows. Given a sequence of policies { π k } k ∈ [ K ] \{\pi^{k}\}_{k\in[K]} , we define the regret as Reg ​ ( K ) = ∑ k = 1 K ( V 1 r k , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r k , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) \mathrm{Reg}(K)=\sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{*}}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big) , and the constraint violation as Vio ​ ( K ) = [ ∑ k = 1 K ( b − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) ] + \mathrm{Vio}(K)=\left[\sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(b-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big)\right]_{+} .

We introduce additional notations as follows. For any V : S → ℝ V:S\to\mathbb{R} , P h ​ V ​ ( s , a ) = ∑ s ′ P h ​ ( s ′ | s , a ) ​ V ​ ( s ′ ) P_{h}V(s,a)=\sum_{s^{\prime}}P_{h}(s^{\prime}|s,a)V(s^{\prime}) and 𝕍 h ​ V ​ ( s , a ) = P h ​ V 2 ​ ( s , a ) − ( P h ​ V ​ ( s , a ) ) 2 \mathbb{V}_{h}V(s,a)=P_{h}V^{2}(s,a)-(P_{h}V(s,a))^{2} . Moreover, we present the Slater assumption, as in [ 37 ] .

Linear Mixture CMDP. Finally, we introduce the definition of linear mixture CMDPs, adapted from [ 15 , 3 , 39 ] , which we assume throughout the paper.

sections.heading: IIIProposed Algorithm
sections.text: In this section, we present our algorithm, called P rimal- D ual P olicy O ptimization W ith B ER nstein bonu S (PD-POWERS, Algorithm 1 ), tailored for finite-horizon linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards and a fixed constraint function. Intuitively, PD-POWERS can be viewed as a primal-dual variant of POWERS, proposed in [ 14 ] for the unconstrained setting.

sections.heading: III-AChallenges in Algorithm Design
sections.text:
To extend an algorithm for the unconstrained problem to the constrained setting, a standard approach is to construct a primal-dual variant. In this framework, the primal variable—maximizing a Lagrangian objective—and the dual variable—balancing reward maximization and constraint satisfaction—are updated alternately.

The main challenge lies in the design of the dual update, as previously proposed dual updates do not directly apply to our setting with adversarial rewards. In the fixed-reward setup, [ 6 , 10 ] adopt a dual update that naturally leads to a strong duality-based analysis. However, such an analysis becomes non-trivial when the rewards vary across episodes.

To address this issue, our key idea is to introduce a regularized dual update, inspired by [ 37 ] . This update enables a drift-based analysis that does not rely on strong duality. In particular, the regularization term induces a negative drift in the dual variable, which plays a crucial role in controlling its growth. This justifies the suitability of our dual update in the adversarial-reward setting. For more details, we refer the reader to III-B and IV-A .

sections.heading: III-BDescription of PD-POWERS
sections.text:
We now describe PD-POWERS. We first introduce the policy optimization step, which combines entropy regularization with policy perturbation. We then present the regularized dual update and discuss its role in controlling the dual variable. Finally, we present the parameter estimation procedure based on weighted ridge regression.

Policy Optimization (Line 3 ).
Our policy optimization step proceeds in the following two steps: π ~ h k − 1 ( ⋅ | s ) ← ( 1 − θ ) π h k − 1 ( ⋅ | s ) + θ π unif ( ⋅ | s ) π h k ( ⋅ | s ) ∝ π ~ h k − 1 ( ⋅ | s ) exp ( α ( Q k − 1 , h r ( s , ⋅ ) + Y k − 1 Q k − 1 , h g ( s , ⋅ ) ) ) \displaystyle\begin{aligned} \widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1}(\cdot|s)&\leftarrow(1-\theta)\pi_{h}^{k-1}(\cdot|s)+\theta\pi_{\mathrm{unif}}(\cdot|s)\\
\pi_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s)&\propto\widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1}(\cdot|s)\exp(\alpha(Q_{{k-1},h}^{r}(s,\cdot)+Y_{k-1}Q_{{k-1},h}^{g}(s,\cdot)))\end{aligned} (3) In the first step of ( 3 ), we define π ~ h k − 1 \widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1} , which is a perturbed version of π h k − 1 \pi_{h}^{k-1} . This step ensures that π ~ h k − 1 ​ ( a | s ) ≥ θ / | A | \widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1}(a|s)\geq\theta/|A| for all a ∈ A a\in A , and hence keeps π ~ h k − 1 \widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1} away from the boundary of the probability simplex. This is essential for controlling the growth of the dual variable. We refer the reader to Section IV for further technical motivation.

Moreover, a key difference from [ 37 ] is that we perturb the policy at every episode. In [ 37 ] , the perturbation is applied every K 3 / 4 K^{3/4} episodes to handle the trade-off between the covering number 3 3 3 Note that [ 37 ] considers linear CMDPs, while our setting is linear mixture CMDPs. In their setting, the covering number of the value function class must be controlled to ensure uniform convergence over all possible value function estimates induced by their algorithm. and the dual variable, which leads to suboptimal dependence on K K in the regret. In contrast, since our setting does not require such covering number arguments, it allows more frequent perturbations and yields optimal dependence on K K .

In the second step of ( 3 ), we perform policy optimization following [ 5 ] . Equivalently, it can be rewritten as the following online mirror descent (OMD) step over the policy space: π h k ( ⋅ | s ) ∈ arg ​ max π ∈ Π ⟨ π ( ⋅ | s ) , Q k − 1 , h r ( s , ⋅ ) + Y k − 1 Q k − 1 , h g ( s , ⋅ ) ⟩ − 1 α D ( π ( ⋅ | s ) | | π ~ h k − 1 ( ⋅ | s ) ) , \pi_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s)\in\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\pi\in\Pi}\langle\pi(\cdot|s),Q_{{k-1},h}^{r}(s,\cdot)+Y_{k-1}Q_{{k-1},h}^{g}(s,\cdot)\rangle-\frac{1}{\alpha}D(\pi(\cdot|s)||\widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1}(\cdot|s)), where D ( ⋅ | | ⋅ ) D(\cdot||\cdot) denotes the KL divergence, and Y k − 1 Y_{k-1} is the dual variable that balances between reward maximization and constraint satisfaction. The KL regularizer encourages the updated policy to remain close to π ~ h k − 1 \widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k-1} .

Regularized Dual Update (Line 4 ).
Next, we present the dual update, inspired by [ 37 ] : Y k ← [ ( 1 − α η H 3 ) Y k − 1 + η ( b − V k − 1 , 1 g ( s 1 ) − α H 3 − 2 θ H 2 ) ] + . \displaystyle\begin{aligned} Y_{k}&\leftarrow\big[(1-\alpha\eta H^{3})Y_{k-1}\\
&\quad+\eta(b-V_{{k-1},1}^{g}(s_{1})-\alpha H^{3}-2\theta H^{2})\big]_{+}.\end{aligned} (4) The dual variable Y k Y_{k} increases when the estimated constraint value falls below the threshold b b and decreases otherwise. Thus, it adaptively balances reward maximization and constraint satisfaction.

For comparison, we recall the dual update used in [ 6 , 10 ] for the fixed-reward setup: Y k ← [ Y k − 1 + η ​ ( b − V k − 1 , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) ) ] [ 0 , 2 γ ] . \displaystyle Y_{k}\leftarrow\left[Y_{k-1}+\eta(b-V_{{k-1},1}^{g}(s_{1}))\right]_{[0,\frac{2}{\gamma}]}. (5) In this update, the dual variable is upper clipped by 2 / γ 2/\gamma to prevent it from diverging. The threshold 2 / γ 2/\gamma is chosen because it serves as a strict upper bound on the optimal dual variable under 1 . In contrast, our update ( 4 ) introduces additional regularizers − α ​ η ​ H 3 ​ Y k − 1 − η ​ ( α ​ H 3 + 2 ​ θ ​ H 2 ) -\alpha\eta H^{3}Y_{k-1}-\eta(\alpha H^{3}+2\theta H^{2}) , instead of relying on upper clipping. Hence, our update does not require knowledge of γ \gamma .

Parameter Estimation (Lines 11 - 19 ).
To obtain tighter confidence intervals, we extend the weighted ridge regression technique—previously used in unconstrained settings [ 39 ] —to our constrained setting. This differs from [ 6 ] , which uses standard ridge regression and does not account for the conditional variance of the next-state value. In contrast, we employ weighted ridge regression–based parameter estimation, where the weights are chosen as estimates of this variance, enabling Bernstein-type concentration inequalities and yielding optimal dependence on H H in the regret bound.

Before describing the weighted ridge regression step, we introduce the basic structure of the Q Q -function estimates, denoted by Q k , h r , Q k , h g Q_{k,h}^{r},Q_{k,h}^{g} . These estimates are computed by backward induction from h = H h=H to 1 1 as follows: Q k , h r ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ← [ r h k ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) + ⟨ θ ^ k , h r , ϕ V k , h + 1 r ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ⟩ + β ^ k ∥ ϕ V k , h + 1 r ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ∥ ( Σ ^ k , h r ) − 1 ] [ 0 , H − h + 1 ] , Q k , h g ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ← [ g h ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) + ⟨ θ ^ k , h g , ϕ V k , h + 1 g ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ⟩ + β ^ k ∥ ϕ V k , h + 1 g ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ∥ ( Σ ^ k , h g ) − 1 ] [ 0 , H − h + 1 ] , \displaystyle\begin{aligned} Q_{k,h}^{r}(\cdot,\cdot)&\leftarrow\bigg[r_{h}^{k}(\cdot,\cdot)+\langle\widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{r},\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{r}}(\cdot,\cdot)\rangle\\
&\qquad\qquad+\widehat{\beta}_{k}\left\|\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{r}}(\cdot,\cdot)\right\|_{(\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{r})^{-1}}\bigg]_{[0,H-h+1]},\\
Q_{k,h}^{g}(\cdot,\cdot)&\leftarrow\bigg[g_{h}(\cdot,\cdot)+\langle\widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{g},\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{g}}(\cdot,\cdot)\rangle\\
&\qquad\qquad+\widehat{\beta}_{k}\left\|\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{g}}(\cdot,\cdot)\right\|_{(\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{g})^{-1}}\bigg]_{[0,H-h+1]},\end{aligned} (6) where the optimistic bonus parameter β ^ k \widehat{\beta}_{k} is given by β ^ k \displaystyle\widehat{\beta}_{k} = 8 ​ d ​ log ⁡ ( 1 + k / λ ) ​ log ⁡ ( 8 ​ H ​ k 2 / δ ) \displaystyle=8\sqrt{d\log(1+k/\lambda)\log(8Hk^{2}/\delta)} + 4 ​ d ​ log ⁡ ( 8 ​ H ​ k 2 / δ ) + λ ​ B \displaystyle\quad+4\sqrt{d}\log(8Hk^{2}/\delta)+\sqrt{\lambda}B

The intuition behind the Q Q -function estimates is as follows. Given a policy π k \pi^{k} , we expect that Q k , h r ​ ( s , a ) ≈ Q h r k , π k ​ ( s , a ) = r h k ​ ( s , a ) + P h ​ V h + 1 r k , π k ​ ( s , a ) Q_{k,h}^{r}(s,a)\approx Q_{h}^{r^{k},\pi^{k}}(s,a)=r_{h}^{k}(s,a)+P_{h}V_{h+1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k}}(s,a) , where the equality follows from the Bellman equation. Assuming θ ^ k , h r ≈ θ h ∗ \widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{r}\approx\theta_{h}^{*} , by the definition of linear mixture CMDPs ( Definition 1 ), we have ⟨ θ ^ k , h r , ϕ V k , h + 1 r ​ ( s , a ) ⟩ ≈ ⟨ θ h ∗ , ϕ V k , h + 1 r ​ ( s , a ) ⟩ = ∑ s ′ ⟨ θ h ∗ , ϕ ​ ( s ′ | s , a ) ⟩ ​ V k , h + 1 r ​ ( s ′ ) = P h ​ V k , h + 1 r ​ ( s , a ) \langle\widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{r},\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{r}}(s,a)\rangle\approx\langle\theta_{h}^{*},\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{r}}(s,a)\rangle=\sum_{s^{\prime}}\langle\theta_{h}^{*},\phi(s^{\prime}|s,a)\rangle V_{k,h+1}^{r}(s^{\prime})=P_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{r}(s,a) . Moreover, β ^ k ​ ‖ ϕ V k , h + 1 r ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ‖ ( Σ ^ k , h r ) − 1 \widehat{\beta}_{k}\|\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{r}}(\cdot,\cdot)\|_{(\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{r})^{-1}} serves as an optimistic bonus term to promote exploration. These arguments validate the design of Q k , h r Q_{k,h}^{r} in ( 6 ), and can be applied to Q k , h g Q_{k,h}^{g} as well.

Now, we present how to obtain θ ^ k , h r \widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{r} and θ ^ k , h g \widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{g} through weighted ridge regression, which approximate θ h ∗ \theta_{h}^{*} . For ℓ ∈ { r , g } \ell\in\{r,g\} , we define θ ^ k , h ℓ ← arg ​ min θ ∈ ℝ d ⁡ λ ​ ‖ θ ‖ 2 2 + ∑ τ = 1 k − 1 [ ⟨ ϕ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) , θ ⟩ − V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h + 1 τ ) ] 2 ( σ ¯ τ , h ℓ ) 2 , \displaystyle\begin{aligned} \widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow&\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\theta\in\mathbb{R}^{d}}\ \lambda\|\theta\|_{2}^{2}\\
&\quad+\sum_{\tau=1}^{k-1}\frac{\big[\langle\phi_{V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau}),\theta\rangle-V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h+1}^{\tau})\big]^{2}}{(\bar{\sigma}_{\tau,h}^{\ell})^{2}},\end{aligned} (7) where ( σ ¯ τ , h ℓ ) 2 (\bar{\sigma}_{\tau,h}^{\ell})^{2} is an upper bound on 𝕍 h ​ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) \mathbb{V}_{h}V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau}) . Equation ( 7 ) admits the following closed form: for ℓ ∈ { r , g } \ell\in\{r,g\} , θ ^ k , h ℓ ← ( Σ ^ k , h ℓ ) − 1 ​ b ^ k , h ℓ , Σ ^ k , h ℓ ← λ ​ I + ∑ τ = 1 k − 1 ( σ ¯ τ , h ℓ ) − 2 ​ ϕ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) ​ ϕ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) ⊤ , b ^ k , h ℓ ← ∑ τ = 1 k − 1 ( σ ¯ τ , h ℓ ) − 2 ​ ϕ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) ​ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h + 1 τ ) . \displaystyle\begin{aligned} &\widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow\left(\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}\right)^{-1}\widehat{b}_{k,h}^{\ell},\\
&\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow\lambda I+\sum_{\tau=1}^{k-1}\left(\bar{\sigma}_{\tau,h}^{\ell}\right)^{-2}\phi_{V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau})\phi_{V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau})^{\top},\\
&\widehat{b}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow\sum_{\tau=1}^{k-1}\left(\bar{\sigma}_{\tau,h}^{\ell}\right)^{-2}\phi_{V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau})V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h+1}^{\tau}).\end{aligned} (8)

We emphasize that the weighted ridge regression in ( 7 ) is useful for handling heteroscedastic noise induced by the time-inhomogeneous transition kernel. Since P h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s , a ) P_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(s,a) is not directly observable, we estimate θ ^ k , h ℓ \widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell} by regressing the samples V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h + 1 τ ) V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h+1}^{\tau}) . This introduces a noise term V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h + 1 τ ) − P h ​ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h+1}^{\tau})-P_{h}V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau}) , whose conditional variance 𝕍 h ​ V τ , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) \mathbb{V}_{h}V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau}) depends on h h , resulting in heteroscedastic noise. In such cases, standard ridge regression is statistically inefficient due to the heteroscedastic structure [ 17 ] . In contrast, weighted ridge regression effectively accounts for this heteroscedasticity [ 39 ] , enabling the use of Bernstein-type concentration inequalities and yielding tighter confidence intervals.

One remaining question for the parameter estimation step is how to construct ( σ ¯ k , h r ) 2 , ( σ ¯ k , h g ) 2 (\bar{\sigma}_{k,h}^{r})^{2},(\bar{\sigma}_{k,h}^{g})^{2} , which are upper bounds on 𝕍 h ​ V k , h + 1 r ​ ( s h k , a h k ) , 𝕍 h ​ V k , h + 1 g ​ ( s h k , a h k ) \mathbb{V}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{r}(s_{h}^{k},a_{h}^{k}),\mathbb{V}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{g}(s_{h}^{k},a_{h}^{k}) , respectively. Again, since P h P_{h} is unknown, we construct the following variance estimate, motivated by the relation that 𝕍 h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s , a ) = ⟨ ϕ ( V k , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s , a ) , θ h ∗ ⟩ − ⟨ ϕ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s , a ) , θ h ∗ ⟩ 2 \mathbb{V}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(s,a)=\langle\phi_{(V_{k,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}}(s,a),\theta_{h}^{*}\rangle-\langle\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}}(s,a),\theta_{h}^{*}\rangle^{2} : for ℓ ∈ { r , g } \ell\in\{r,g\} , 𝕍 ¯ h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) = [ ⟨ ϕ ( V k , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 , θ ~ k , h ℓ ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ⟩ ] [ 0 , H 2 ] − [ ⟨ ϕ V k , h + 1 ℓ , θ ^ k , h ℓ ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) ⟩ ] [ 0 , H ] 2 . \displaystyle\begin{aligned} &\bar{\mathbb{V}}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(\cdot,\cdot)=\left[\langle\phi_{(V_{k,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}},\widetilde{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}(\cdot,\cdot)\rangle\right]_{\left[0,H^{2}\right]}\\
&\qquad\qquad\qquad-\left[\langle\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}},\widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}(\cdot,\cdot)\rangle\right]_{\left[0,H\right]}^{2}.\end{aligned} (9) where θ ^ k , h ℓ \widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell} is defined in ( 8 ) and θ ~ k , h ℓ ← ( Σ ~ k , h ℓ ) − 1 ​ b ~ k , h ℓ , Σ ~ k , h ℓ ← λ ​ I + ∑ τ = 1 k − 1 ϕ ( V τ , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) ​ ϕ ( V τ , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) ⊤ , b ~ k , h ℓ ← ∑ τ = 1 k − 1 ϕ ( V τ , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s h τ , a h τ ) ​ ( V τ , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s h + 1 τ ) . \displaystyle\begin{aligned} &\widetilde{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow\left(\widetilde{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}\right)^{-1}\widetilde{b}_{k,h}^{\ell},\\
&\widetilde{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow\lambda I+\sum_{\tau=1}^{k-1}\phi_{(V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau})\phi_{(V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau})^{\top},\\
&\widetilde{b}_{k,h}^{\ell}\leftarrow\sum_{\tau=1}^{k-1}\phi_{(V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}}(s_{h}^{\tau},a_{h}^{\tau})(V_{\tau,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}(s_{h+1}^{\tau}).\end{aligned} (10) Note that ( 10 ) is a consequence of standard ridge regression with respect to ( V k , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 (V_{k,h+1}^{\ell})^{2} , so that ⟨ ϕ ( V k , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 , θ ~ k , h ℓ ​ ( s , a ) ⟩ ≈ P h ​ ( V k , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s , a ) \langle\phi_{(V_{k,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}},\widetilde{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}(s,a)\rangle\approx P_{h}(V_{k,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}(s,a) . Moreover, the following proposition characterizes the discrepancy between the variance estimated in ( 9 ) and its true value 𝕍 h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( ⋅ , ⋅ ) \mathbb{V}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(\cdot,\cdot) .

Combining this with Theorem 4.1 in [ 39 ] implies that ‖ ( θ ~ k , h ℓ − θ h ∗ ) ‖ Σ ~ k , h ℓ ≤ β ~ k , ‖ θ ^ k , h ℓ − θ h ∗ ‖ Σ ^ k , h ℓ ≤ β ˇ k , \|(\widetilde{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}-\theta_{h}^{*})\|_{\widetilde{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}}\leq\widetilde{\beta}_{k},\ \|\widehat{\theta}_{k,h}^{\ell}-\theta_{h}^{*}\|_{\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}}\leq\check{\beta}_{k}, where β ~ k \displaystyle\widetilde{\beta}_{k} = 8 ​ H 2 ​ d ​ log ⁡ ( 1 + k ​ H 4 / ( d ​ λ ) ) ​ log ⁡ ( 8 ​ H ​ k 2 / δ ) \displaystyle=8H^{2}\sqrt{d\log(1+kH^{4}/(d\lambda))\log(8Hk^{2}/\delta)} + 4 ​ H 2 ​ log ⁡ ( 8 ​ H ​ k 2 / δ ) + λ ​ B , \displaystyle\quad+4H^{2}\log(8Hk^{2}/\delta)+\sqrt{\lambda}B, β ˇ k \displaystyle\check{\beta}_{k} = 8 ​ d ​ log ⁡ ( 1 + k / λ ) ​ log ⁡ ( 8 ​ H ​ k 2 / δ ) \displaystyle=8d\sqrt{\log(1+k/\lambda)\log(8Hk^{2}/\delta)} + 4 ​ d ​ log ⁡ ( 8 ​ H ​ k 2 / δ ) + λ ​ B . \displaystyle\quad+4\sqrt{d}\log(8Hk^{2}/\delta)+\sqrt{\lambda}B.

Based on these observations, we have | 𝕍 ¯ h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s , a ) − 𝕍 h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s , a ) | ≤ E k , h ℓ |\bar{\mathbb{V}}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(s,a)-\mathbb{V}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(s,a)|\leq E_{k,h}^{\ell} , where the offset term E k , h ℓ E_{k,h}^{\ell} is defined as follows: for ℓ ∈ { r , g } \ell\in\{r,g\} , E k , h ℓ = min ⁡ { H 2 , β ~ k ​ ‖ ϕ ( V k , h + 1 ℓ ) 2 ​ ( s , a ) ‖ ( Σ ~ k , h ℓ ) − 1 } + min ⁡ { H 2 , 2 ​ H ​ β ˇ k ​ ‖ ϕ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s , a ) ‖ ( Σ ^ k , h ℓ ) − 1 } . \displaystyle\begin{aligned} E_{k,h}^{\ell}&=\min\left\{H^{2},\widetilde{\beta}_{k}\left\|\phi_{(V_{k,h+1}^{\ell})^{2}}(s,a)\right\|_{(\widetilde{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell})^{-1}}\right\}\\
&\quad+\min\left\{H^{2},2H\check{\beta}_{k}\left\|\phi_{V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}}(s,a)\right\|_{\left(\widehat{\Sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell}\right)^{-1}}\right\}.\end{aligned} (11) Finally, we present σ ¯ k , h r \bar{\sigma}_{k,h}^{r} and σ ¯ k , h g \bar{\sigma}_{k,h}^{g} , defined for ℓ ∈ { r , g } \ell\in\{r,g\} as ( σ ¯ k , h ℓ ) 2 = max ⁡ { H 2 / d , 𝕍 ¯ h ​ V k , h + 1 ℓ ​ ( s h k , a h k ) + E k , h ℓ } . \displaystyle(\bar{\sigma}_{k,h}^{\ell})^{2}=\max\left\{H^{2}/d,\bar{\mathbb{V}}_{h}V_{k,h+1}^{\ell}(s_{h}^{k},a_{h}^{k})+E_{k,h}^{\ell}\right\}. (12)

The effectiveness of our estimates is supported by the following lemma.

sections.heading: IVAnalysis
sections.text: In this section, we present our main result ( Theorem 1 ), which establishes upper bounds on both the regret and the constraint violation. We also provide a high-level overview of the analysis; the full proofs are deferred to the appendix.

sections.heading: IV-ALimitations of Strong Duality-Based Analysis
sections.text:
Before presenting the overview of our analysis, we explain why the strong duality-based approach induced by ( 5 ) fails when the rewards vary across episodes.

On top of ( 5 ), the strong duality–based analysis [ 6 , 10 ] begins by upper bounding the following composite regret term for any Y ∈ [ 0 , 2 / γ ] Y\in[0,2/\gamma] : ∑ k = 1 K ( V 1 r , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) + Y ​ ∑ k = 1 K ( b − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) . \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(V_{1}^{r,\pi^{*}}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big)+Y\sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(b-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big). (13)

The next step is to separate the composite regret into regret and violation terms. Here, [ 7 ] shows that the following holds under strong duality: for an optimal policy π ∗ ∈ arg ​ max π ⁡ V 1 r , π ​ ( s 1 ) ​ s.t. ​ V 1 g , π ​ ( s 1 ) ≥ b \pi^{*}\in\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\pi}V_{1}^{r,\pi}(s_{1})\ \textnormal{s.t.}\ V_{1}^{g,\pi}(s_{1})\geq b and some Δ k \Delta_{k} , if V 1 r , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r , π k ​ ( s 1 ) + 2 γ ​ ( b − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) ≤ Δ k , V_{1}^{r,\pi^{*}}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})+\frac{2}{\gamma}\big(b-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big)\leq\Delta_{k}, (14) then V 1 r , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ≤ Δ k and b − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ≤ γ 2 ​ Δ k . V_{1}^{r,\pi^{*}}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\leq\Delta_{k}\ \ \text{and}\ \ b-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\leq\frac{\gamma}{2}\Delta_{k}. (15) Applying this argument for all k ∈ [ K ] k\in[K] yields regret and violation bounds from a bound on ( 13 ).

Now, let us examine whether the same argument can be applied in our setting. Since the rewards change over k k , the analogue of ( 14 ) becomes V 1 r k , π k , ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r k , π k ​ ( s 1 ) + 2 γ ​ ( b − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) ≤ Δ k , V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k,*}}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k}}(s_{1})+\frac{2}{\gamma}\big(b-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big)\leq\Delta_{k}, where π k , ∗ ∈ arg ​ max π ⁡ V 1 r k , π ​ ( s 1 ) s.t. V 1 g , π ​ ( s 1 ) ≥ b \pi^{k,*}\in\operatorname*{arg\,max}_{\pi}V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi}(s_{1})\ \ \textnormal{s.t.}\ \ V_{1}^{g,\pi}(s_{1})\geq b for each k k .
Consequently, as the analogue of ( 13 ), the corresponding composite regret must use the sequence { π k , ∗ } k ∈ [ K ] \{\pi^{k,*}\}_{k\in[K]} as comparator policies, namely, ∑ k = 1 K ( V 1 r k , π k , ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r k , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) + Y ​ ∑ k = 1 K ( b − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) . \sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k,*}}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big)+Y\sum_{k=1}^{K}\big(b-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\big). However, obtaining an upper bound on this term is non-trivial unless r k r^{k} is known before episode k k , which is not allowed in the adversarial-reward setting.
This highlights the limitations of strong duality-based analysis in our setting.

sections.heading: IV-BOur Analysis
sections.text:
To overcome these limitations, we establish a drift-based analysis based on ( 4 ), which does not rely on strong duality. As a first step, we provide decompositions of Reg ​ ( K ) \mathrm{Reg}(K) and Vio ​ ( K ) \mathrm{Vio}(K) .
Since the optimal policy π ∗ \pi^{*} satisfies V 1 g , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) ≥ b V_{1}^{g,\pi^{*}}(s_{1})\geq b and Y k ≥ 0 Y_{k}\geq 0 , we have Reg ​ ( K ) \displaystyle\mathrm{Reg}(K) ≤ ∑ k = 1 K ( V 1 r k , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) + Y k ​ V 1 g , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V k , 1 r ​ ( s 1 ) − Y k ​ V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) ) \displaystyle\leq\sum_{k=1}^{K}\left(V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{*}}(s_{1})+Y_{k}V_{1}^{g,\pi^{*}}(s_{1})-V_{k,1}^{r}(s_{1})-Y_{k}V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})\right) + ∑ k = 1 K ( V k , 1 r ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 r k , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) + ∑ k = 1 K Y k ​ ( V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) − b ) , \displaystyle\quad+\sum_{k=1}^{K}\left(V_{k,1}^{r}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{k}}(s_{1})\right)+\sum_{k=1}^{K}Y_{k}\left(V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})-b\right), Vio ​ ( K ) = ∑ k = 1 K ( b − V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) ) + ∑ k = 1 K ( V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) − V 1 g , π k ​ ( s 1 ) ) . \displaystyle\mathrm{Vio}(K)=\sum_{k=1}^{K}(b-V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1}))+\sum_{k=1}^{K}(V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})-V_{1}^{g,\pi^{k}}(s_{1})).

Since we employ weighted ridge regression for parameter estimation,
the bias terms appearing in both the regret and violation decompositions admit the following bounds.

Next, we focus on ∑ k = 1 K ( b − V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) ) \sum_{k=1}^{K}(b-V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})) . By the dual update, ∑ k = 1 K ( b − V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) ) \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{K}(b-V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})) ≤ Y K + 1 η + α ​ H 3 ​ ∑ k = 1 K Y k + α ​ K ​ H 3 + 2 ​ θ ​ K ​ H 2 . \displaystyle\leq\frac{Y_{K+1}}{\eta}+\alpha H^{3}\sum_{k=1}^{K}Y_{k}+\alpha KH^{3}+2\theta KH^{2}. Therefore, it suffices to bound Y k Y_{k} uniformly over k k . To this end, we employ drift-based arguments [ 36 , 34 ] .
Based on the observation that our regularizer in ( 4 ) satisfies − α ​ η ​ H 2 ​ ( 1 + Y k ) − 2 ​ η ​ θ ​ H ≤ η ​ ⟨ π h k + 1 − π h k , Q k , h g ⟩ -\alpha\eta H^{2}(1+Y_{k})-2\eta\theta H\leq\eta\langle\pi_{h}^{k+1}-\pi_{h}^{k},Q_{k,h}^{g}\rangle ( Lemma 18 ), we establish the following drift inequality: Y k + 1 2 − Y k 2 2 ≤ − γ ​ η ​ Y k + C \displaystyle\frac{Y_{k+1}^{2}-Y_{k}^{2}}{2}\leq-\gamma\eta Y_{k}+C (16) + η α 𝔼 π ¯ [ ∑ h = 1 H D ( π ¯ h ( ⋅ | s h ) | | π ~ h k ( ⋅ | s h ) ) − D ( π ¯ h ( ⋅ | s h ) | | π h k + 1 ( ⋅ | s h ) ) ] \displaystyle+\frac{\eta}{\alpha}\mathbb{E}_{\bar{\pi}}\left[\sum_{h=1}^{H}D(\bar{\pi}_{h}(\cdot|s_{h})||\widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s_{h}))-D(\bar{\pi}_{h}(\cdot|s_{h})||\pi_{h}^{k+1}(\cdot|s_{h}))\right] where C = α ​ η ​ H 3 2 + 2 ​ η ​ H 2 ​ θ + 2 ​ η ​ H 2 + 2 ​ η 2 ​ ( H 2 + α 2 ​ H 6 + 9 ​ η 2 ​ α 2 ​ H 8 ​ K 2 + 4 ​ θ 2 ​ H 4 ) . C=\frac{\alpha\eta H^{3}}{2}+2\eta H^{2}\theta+2\eta H^{2}+2\eta^{2}(H^{2}+\alpha^{2}H^{6}+9\eta^{2}\alpha^{2}H^{8}K^{2}+4\theta^{2}H^{4}).

From ( 16 ), we observe that π ~ h k \widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k} is a perturbed policy, and hence the KL divergence term D ( π ¯ h ( ⋅ | s h ) ∥ π ~ h k ( ⋅ | s h ) ) D(\bar{\pi}_{h}(\cdot|s_{h})\|\widetilde{\pi}_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s_{h})) admits a small upper bound. This highlights the necessity of the policy perturbation step. Without this perturbation, the KL divergence term D ( π ¯ h ( ⋅ | s h ) ∥ π h k ( ⋅ | s h ) ) D(\bar{\pi}_{h}(\cdot|s_{h})\|\pi_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s_{h})) could be unbounded, as π h k \pi_{h}^{k} approaches the boundary of the simplex. With further analysis, we obtain the following bound.

Since Y k Y_{k} is bounded by Lemma 3 , it follows that ∑ k = 1 K Y k ​ ( V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) − b ) = O ~ ​ ( H ​ K + H 5 ​ K / γ 2 ) . \sum_{k=1}^{K}Y_{k}\left(V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})-b\right)=\widetilde{{O}}\left(H\sqrt{K}+H^{5}\sqrt{K}/\gamma^{2}\right). The detailed derivation is deferred to the appendix.

To complete the analysis, it remains to bound ∑ k = 1 K ( V 1 r k , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) + Y k ​ V 1 g , π ∗ ​ ( s 1 ) − V k , 1 r ​ ( s 1 ) − Y k ​ V k , 1 g ​ ( s 1 ) ) \sum_{k=1}^{K}\left(V_{1}^{r^{k},\pi^{*}}(s_{1})+Y_{k}V_{1}^{g,\pi^{*}}(s_{1})-V_{k,1}^{r}(s_{1})-Y_{k}V_{k,1}^{g}(s_{1})\right) . By Lemma 1 and the value difference lemma (Lemma 1 of [ 25 ] ), this term can be bounded as ∑ k = 1 K 𝔼 π ∗ [ ∑ h = 1 H ⟨ Q k , h r ( s h , ⋅ ) + Y k Q k , h g ( s h , ⋅ ) , π h ∗ ( ⋅ | s h ) − π h k ( ⋅ | s h ) ⟩ | s 1 ] . \sum_{k=1}^{K}\mathbb{E}_{\pi^{*}}\left[\sum_{h=1}^{H}\langle Q_{k,h}^{r}(s_{h},\cdot)+Y_{k}Q_{k,h}^{g}(s_{h},\cdot),\pi_{h}^{*}(\cdot|s_{h})-\pi_{h}^{k}(\cdot|s_{h})\rangle|s_{1}\right]. Moreover, since we employ the policy optimization ( 3 ), the above term can be effectively controlled. Specifically, we apply the standard OMD lemma [ 13 ] , together with the policy perturbation step. Combining these ingredients, we obtain the following lemma.

sections.heading: VNumerical Experiments
sections.text:
In Figure 1 , we evaluate PD-POWERS on a CMDP instance adapted from [ 14 ] , with modifications including the addition of a constraint. The details of the setting are described below. We conduct 5 5 simulations with K = 2 , 000 K=2{,}000 under different random seeds. Each plot shows the average, and the shaded regions indicate 95 95 % confidence intervals.

As in [ 14 ] , we compare both the regret and violation of our algorithm with those of a random policy—one that selects a uniformly sampled action at each step. As shown in the figure, our algorithm exhibits sublinear growth in both regret and violation with respect to the number of episodes, while those of the random policy grow linearly. Therefore, the numerical results support our theoretical findings.

Let H = 10 H=10 and d = 5 d=5 . Let S = { 0 , … , H + 1 } S=\{0,\ldots,H+1\} and A = { − 1 , 1 } d − 1 A=\{-1,1\}^{d-1} . Let s = 0 s=0 be the initial state and b = 6 b=6 . For s < H s<H , we define P h ​ ( s + 1 | s , a ) = − 0.01 ⋅ 𝟏 ⊤ ​ a + 0.95 P_{h}(s+1|s,a)=-0.01\cdot\bm{1}^{\top}a+0.95 and P h ​ ( H + 1 | s , a ) = 1 − P h ​ ( s + 1 | s , a ) P_{h}(H+1|s,a)=1-P_{h}(s+1|s,a) . For s = H s=H or H + 1 H+1 , we set P h ​ ( s | s , a ) = 1 P_{h}(s|s,a)=1 . For s < H s<H , if ⌊ k / 10 ⌋ ≡ 0 mod 2 \lfloor k/10\rfloor\equiv 0\mod 2 , then r k ​ ( s , a ) = 0.4 ​ ∑ i = 1 d − 1 a i + 1 2 ​ ( d − 1 ) r_{k}(s,a)=0.4\sum_{i=1}^{d-1}\frac{a_{i}+1}{2(d-1)} , and if ⌊ k / 10 ⌋ ≡ 1 mod 2 \lfloor k/10\rfloor\equiv 1\mod 2 , then r k ​ ( s , a ) = 0.4 ​ ( 1 − ∑ i = 1 d − 1 a i + 1 2 ​ ( d − 1 ) ) r_{k}(s,a)=0.4(1-\sum_{i=1}^{d-1}\frac{a_{i}+1}{2(d-1)}) . For all k ∈ [ K ] k\in[K] , r k ​ ( H , a ) = 0 r_{k}(H,a)=0 and r k ​ ( H + 1 , a ) = 1 r_{k}(H+1,a)=1 . Let g ​ ( s , a ) = ∑ i = 1 d − 1 a i + 1 2 ​ ( d − 1 ) g(s,a)=\sum_{i=1}^{d-1}\frac{a_{i}+1}{2(d-1)} if s < H s<H , and g ​ ( s , a ) = 0 g(s,a)=0 otherwise. The code is available at: https://github.com/kihyun-yu/pd-powers.

sections.heading: VIConclusion
sections.text: In this paper, we study online linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards under full-information feedback and a fixed constraint function. We propose PD-POWERS, a primal-dual policy optimization algorithm combining a regularized dual update and weighted ridge regression-based parameter estimation. Moreover, we show that PD-POWERS achieves a near-optimal regret bound. Despite these results, several limitations remain. First, when the integration oracle 𝒪 {\mathcal{O}} is not available, the computational complexity may depend on | S | |S| , making the algorithm inefficient for large state spaces. Second, the regret and constraint violation bounds become large when γ ≪ 1 \gamma\ll 1 . Therefore, developing algorithms that remain robust under such degenerate conditions is an important direction for future work.

figures.caption: Figure 1 : Regret and constraint violation of PD-POWERS.
